title: Upper and Lower Bounds for the Linear Ordering Principle
speaker: Ilya Volkovich
abstract:
The Linear Ordering Principle (LOP) is a total search problem that generalizes the task of finding the minimum element of a given order to settings in which the order need not be total. Building on this, Korten and Pitassi (FOCS 2024) introduced the complexity class L2P as the polynomial-time Turing closure of LOP. They also showed that L2P  lies between MA (Merlin–Arthur protocols) and S2P (the second symmetric level of the polynomial hierarchy).
In this work, we establish tighter upper and lower bounds for L2P by sandwiching it between PprMA and PprSBP. Here, the oracles are promise problems, and SBP is the only currently known natural complexity class that lies between MA and AM. The containment L2P⊆PprSBP is obtained via an iterative procedure that uses a prSBP oracle to estimate the average order rank of a subset and to identify the minimum element of a linear order. This technique may be of independent interest.
These containment results yield several consequences: 
We answer affirmatively an open question posed by Chakaravarthy and Roy (Computational Complexity, 2011) on whether PprMA⊆S2P, thereby settling the relative standing of previously existing Karp–Lipton–style collapse results.
We resolve an open question of Korten and Pitassi whether a Karp–Lipton–style collapse can be established for L2P.
Based on a joint work with Edward Hirsch.

proof_stages.label: TFNP, LOP, and the class L2P
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up total polynomially verifiable search problems, defines the linear ordering principle as the task of finding either a minimum or a violation of a purported strict linear order, and defines L2P via reductions to LOP, including closure properties and NP containment.

proof_stages.label: Range avoidance and known upper bounds
proof_stages.summary: The range-avoidance problem AVOID is introduced as another total search problem, its importance for explicit pseudorandom constructions is explained, and the known progression of upper bounds from randomized oracle bounds through S2P to L2P is reviewed.

proof_stages.label: MA inside L2P via PRGs and AVOID
proof_stages.summary: Using hardness-versus-randomness, MA verification is decoupled into nondeterminism plus pseudorandomness, then PRGs are reduced to AVOID and AVOID is implemented in L2P, yielding MA contained in L2P.

proof_stages.label: S2P benchmark and Karp-Lipton motivation
proof_stages.summary: S2P is defined through the irrefutable-certificate complete problem, compared with L2P in terms of closure, containment, upper and lower bounds, and then the Karp-Lipton collapse question is identified as the apparent remaining distinction between S2P and L2P.

proof_stages.label: Main L2P sandwich theorem
proof_stages.summary: The main results are stated: L2P contains P with a PromiseMA oracle and is contained in P with a PromiseSBP oracle, placing L2P between known MA/SBP/AM-style bounds and giving immediate consequences such as resolving a Chakaravarthy-Roy open problem and producing exponential lower bounds for E with a PromiseSBP oracle.

proof_stages.label: Promise-MA lower-bound argument and Karp-Lipton alignment
proof_stages.summary: The proof of MA contained in L2P is observed to be promise-robust, so together with L2P's Turing-closure it gives P^PromiseMA contained in L2P and thereby extends and linearly organizes the known Karp-Lipton collapse classes through L2P.

proof_stages.label: Promise-SBP upper bound via approximate rank descent
proof_stages.summary: SBP is recast through the set-size-estimation complete problem, PromiseSBP oracles are used for approximate counting and approximate order-rank estimation, and an iterative bit-restriction argument repeatedly lowers average rank until a minimum element of the linear order is found.

proof_stages.label: Limits, open comparisons, and Q&A
proof_stages.summary: The talk concludes with open problems about making the PromiseSBP queries nonadaptive, comparing L2P with S2P and PromiseSBP with S2P, relating this upper bound to UEOPL-style TFNP bounds, and interpreting assumptions about NP machines with subexponentially many accepting paths.

examples.name: Integer factorization as a TFNP problem
examples.note: Integer factorization illustrates a total search problem whose solutions are guaranteed by the fundamental theorem of arithmetic but may be hard to find efficiently.
examples.engagement_reason: Integer factorization is cited as a natural TFNP problem with the fundamental-theorem guarantee, but no algorithm or computation is carried out on it.

examples.name: Constant-zero comparison circuit
examples.note: The audience's constant-zero circuit example makes concrete how a purported comparison relation can fail linearity by leaving elements incomparable.
examples.engagement_reason: The constant-zero comparison circuit is evaluated as making all pairs incomparable, hence producing an LOP violation.

passes.reason: the constant-zero comparison circuit is the empty/trivial relation, invariant under all inputs, and is used to show a purported comparison circuit may fail to be a linear order
The all-zero comparator is the empty minimal comparison relation and is used to show comparability or linearity can fail.
The all-zero comparison circuit is a trivial empty relation used to refute the suggestion that circuit-given comparisons cannot have incomparability violations.
the all-zero comparator is a trivial maximally uniform no-comparison relation used to exhibit an LOP violation

the all-zero comparator is an empty/minimal relation used to refute the claim that incomparability cannot occur
The all-zero comparison is an empty/collapsed and maximally invariant relation, used to show that a circuit can fail to define a linear order.
The all-zero comparison circuit is a trivial empty/incomparable relation used to show that circuit-given comparisons can violate linear order.
